headlines.title: SULZER MAY MAKE RACE FOR ASSEMBLY AS A PROGRESSIVE
headlines.subtitle: Sign This Blank for Better Baby
headlines.fullText: NEW YORK, Oct. 18,-William Sulzer may take the back trail" to continue the fight against his enemies. Impeached as governor and forcibly removed from office it is said he will now become the Progressive candidate for the assembly from the Sixth of New York. This offer was made to the impeached governor late Friday by Max Gestuan from the Sixth district. He said Sulzer promised to run if he found the people wanted him to do so. The will be nominated by his own story. The first statement since his conviction was made Friday night and in part is: "The impropriety of senators who were de to the judgment, because had they refused to vote-as a sense of decency should have induced them to do I would not have been convicted on any of the articles of impeachment. Mr. Murphy controlled the assembly and ordered the impeachment procedure and wrote the judgment. "The meetings of the court were in secret and behind closed doors. It was a star chamber proceeding, which the agents of the state could work for my conviction undiscovered. "High Court of Infamy." "They called it the high court of impeachment, but history will call it Murphy's high court of infamy. "When I declined to obey the orders of the 'boss' about patronage; when I refused to call off Hennessy and prevent further investigation of graft and finally, when I set in motion the wires of the machinery of justice and to stop the looting of the state then and not until then did Mr. Murphy threaten me with destruction. That is why I am here today. I will go into details later on. "Before I was a candidate for the governorship I was in debt about $70,000 and had more assets than I have at present. "The truth is, I owe now as follows: I. M. Josephthal, $21,000; J. F. Meany, $3,000; smaller creditors, about $1,000, making a total of $76,000, besides the cost of my trial. "I own no real estate, no mortgages, no stocks or bonds and no other assets save an equity in some stock in two small mining companies. This mining stock has no market value. I have been unfortunate in mining matters and, in so far as I could, got out of them last year. "I had on deposit in the Farmers Loan and Trust Company before I deposit there now not much more and all in all, I am poorer today than I was before the fight for the governorship began last fall." to prevent further disclosures and prosecutions. I became satisfied it was useless to present myself as a witness because the defense that I could make, which I considered my best and most efficient means of clearing my name, would not be permitted to be told by me in court. "Let me say here, that I wanted to take the witness stand in my own behalf-especially to tell the story of my troubles with the "boss" and to surd story of Allan A. Ryan and to ex- "Peck Lied." "Those familiar with the facts can testify that Peck lied about me to save his job; that Morgenthau was fooled by the clever ruse of an unscrupulous enemy; and that Allan Ryan was in Albany several days under the tutelage of astute counsel to aid the prosecution at the psychological moment, in any way desired. "Suffice it to say that Allan Ryan came to see me; I did not go to see him and I never asked him or anyone else to request Senator Root or De Lancey Nicoll to see Mr. Barnes or Mr. Murphy- or anyone else, for that matter. clude the evidence of Mr. Hennessy's efforts which would discredit the testimony of Mr. Peck and show his motive for telling an untruth, which what has been discovered in the way of crime and corruption in the various departments of the state government and which were largely the inducing causes in bringing about my impeachment in order to prevent further disclosures and prosecutions. I became satisfied it was useless to present myself as a witness because the defense that I could make, which I considered my best and most efficient means of clearing my name, would not be permitted to be told by me in court. "Another thing. Every dollar given me which I deposited to my personal or to an agent of Mr. Murphy or his this is known. I will go into details later on.

headlines.title: Ellis Island 'Prison' of Many Notables
headlines.fullText: Some of the notables who have occupied "cells" on New York's famous Ellis Island, where "undesirable immigrants" are sent pending deportation. Mrs. Pankhurst is there now. Above to the left is shown Prince Pignatelli d'Aragona, as he was photographed leaning over the side of the ferry which conveys immigrants from the island. Sprang Castro, former president of Venezuela, was taken in his "cell" on the island. To the right is Miss Marie Lloyd, the English music hall singer who recently spent several unpleasant days as a guest of the United States commissioner of immigration.

headlines.title: BLANKE REFUSES TO TAKE PLACE ON RESERVOIR BOARD
headlines.fullText: Water Commissioner Blanke refused a place on the West Fork reservoir committee Saturday, when Finance Commissioner Smith offered to resign from the committee in favor of Blanke. Smith's offer followed a lengthy discussion of a letter received by Mayor Milam from the board of completion of the dam by day labor. Commissioner Houth offered to appoint Commissioner Blanke on the committee. Blanke gave as an excuse more work than he can handle in looking after the pumping plants. The reservoir committee is composed of Mayor Milam and Commissioners Allen and Smith.

headlines.title: ROBERTSON TO BE K. C. HORSE SHOW JUDGE
headlines.subtitle: Is First Fort Worth Man to Officiate in Kansas City or Chicago.
headlines.fullText: I. P. Robertson of Fort Worth left for Kansas City Saturday to act as one of the judges in the horse show to be held there all of next week. Robertson is the first Fort Worth man ever to act as judge in either the great horse shows of Kansas City or Chicago, though he has shown a number alone in the roadster classes. He has been invited.

headlines.title: CAMPBELL TO ENTER SENATORIAL CONTEST?
headlines.subtitle: Former Governor Tells Congressmen He Will Run for Office.
headlines.fullText: WASHINGTON, Oct. 18,-Former Governor of Texas T. M. Campbell was here yesterday and informed several congressmen that he would enter the race for United States senator. He has long refused to comment on the question. He is en route to Palestine from New York.

headlines.title: ALL ANGLERS WELCOME TO MINNETONKA FISH
headlines.subtitle: City Commission Refuses to Lease Island to Gun Club.
headlines.fullText: The waters of Lake Minnetonka will remain the property of the piscatorial populace. The Fort Worth Rod and Gun Club appeared before the city commission Saturday and asked that it be granted a lease on Minnetonka as a fishing and hunting club. T. L. Hartshorn, manufacturer, president of the club, presented a petition asking for a lease for a term of years. He told the commission that the island He said also if there were to be any privileges granted they would have to be made at once so clubhouses could be erected before the water got higher. Mayor Milam did not believe favor should be granted any set of citizens and Commissioner Louis Hanke wanted himself put on record as being opposed to leasing any lands in connection with the reservoir. These island, until the proposition had been thoroughly investigated. He declared the reservoir property belonged to all of the people. Hartshorn explained that fish were being dynamited and many people were hunting with rifles on the line. The commission asked Hartshorn if he would desire to seek special privileges.

headlines.title: Brief Items of Interest in City
headlines.fullText:
Charged with the illicit sale of liquor, Bob Merril of Electra, was bound over to the United States grand jury Friday by Commissioner G. W. Mitchell. Merrill was arrested and brought to Fort Worth by United States Marshal McDonald.
Gospel Luncheon Postponed. The gospel luncheon which was to have been held Saturday as had been announced. It will be held one day next week.
Deaths
Simon-Ben Simon, 29, died early Saturday. The services were held Saturday by the Fort Worth Undertaking Company. Burial was in Oakwood cemetery. A mother and sister survive him.
Black-Mrs. Ida Black, 49, died Saturday at a local sanitarium. The body will be forwarded to Boyd, Texas, for burial by Undertaker Samuel D. Sloan.

headlines.title: 7 HURT IN WRECK
headlines.fullText: LITTLE ROCK, Ark., Oct. 18- Train No. 44, Chicago, Rock Island & Pacific, running between Sayre, Okla., and Memphis, Tenn., was wrecked one mile east of Biscoe at 2:50 a. m. today. Reports state there were seven persons injured but none seriously.

headlines.title: Another "Trust" Named.
headlines.fullText: Another "Trust" Named. The American Cotton Bagging trust is responsible for the great increase in price of bagging in recent years, according to Secretary Redfield, who issued a statement today that the high price of raw jute and the absolute control of cotton bagging by the American Cotton Bagging Manufacturing Company and the Ludlow Manufacturing Association were the cause.

headlines.title: Battleships in Battle Practice.
headlines.fullText: NORFOLK, VA., Oct. 18-The Atlantic fleet of twenty-one battleships put out to heavy battle practice today, with Secretaries Daniels, Garrison, Redfield and a distinguished company from official circles in Washington.

headlines.title: 500 DELEGATES AT WATERWAY MEETING
headlines.fullText: ORANGE, Texas, Oct. 18 .- The Interstate Inland Waterway League of Texas and Louisiana convened here yesterday, 500 delegates being present. President C. S. Holland of Victoria presided. The following officers were elected: C. S. Holland, president, Victoria; Henry L. Gueydan, vice president, Gueydan, La.; C. J. Palmer, vice president, Houston; Leon Locke, general secretary, Lake Charles, La.; Roy Miller, secretary for Texas, Corpus Christi. Executive committee, C. S. Holland, Victoria; C. J. Palmer, Houston; Van Tyne, Port Arthur; C. B. Culver, Matagorda; W. S. Holman, Bay City; C. Collins, Brownsville; J. S. Cullinan, Houston; Henri L. Gueydan, Gueydan, La.; Leon Locke, Lake Charles, La.; I. A. Pharr, Morgan City; H. L. Hughes, Jeanette; J. G. Medlenka, Crowley; J. H. Jastremski, Houma; D. L. McPherson, Abbeville; Warren B. Reed, New Orleans; T. Barton, Albemarle; Felix Jackson, Vinton; John H. Shary, Mission; H. J. Bolton, Wharton; H. L. Dean, Palacios; Lindsey Waters, Harlingen and Brownsville; F. H. Farwell, Orange; Peter S. Lawton, New Iberia; C. O. Mouton, Lafayette; Dr. Mattie Lyons, Sulphur; L. S. Mims, Freeport; J. A. Wakefield, Cameron; Horace H. Harvey, Harvey; C. S. Williams, Patterson; William Ditch, Morgan City.

headlines.title: ST. ANDREW'S WOMEN PLAN MONTHLY MARKET
headlines.fullText: Because of the success of a market conducted by the women of St. Andrew's church Saturday, it was decided to hold a monthly market, for which orders for all kinds of cakes, pies and candies will be taken in advance, to be delivered the day of sale, is planned. Members of the parish responded with bread, candy and other homemade dainties, which found a ready sale Saturday morning. The market will close tonight. The money will be used to meet several obligations. The market is in charge of Mrs. George I. Abbott, Mrs. David Hughes and Mrs. T. A. Darling. Others are assisting.

headlines.title: Wilson Nomina President Nominates.
headlines.fullText: President Wilson today sent to the senate the following nominations: Governor of Porto Rico, Arthur Yager of Kentucky; solicitor of internal revenue, Ellis C. Johnson of Washington; United States marshal Southern district of Texas, J. A. Williams.
